topic: Two friends realize they remember a shared event completely differently
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam slapped his forehead with the heel of his hand, the sound sharp in the quiet café. "Seriously, Maya? We are going over this *again*?" He ran a hand through his already messy hair, his jaw tight. He stared at the chipped ceramic mug in front of him, the steam doing nothing to soothe the rising tension in his chest. "I distinctly remember you being the one who tripped over the curb right before the fireworks started. You were flailing, apologizing like a total idiot." Maya just blinked, a slow, unreadable expression settling on her face. "That’s not what I recall, Liam. I remember *you* stumbling, nearly dropping your entire bag of snacks. You looked completely mortified." Liam leaned back, the chair groaning under his weight. He let out a slow, heavy sigh, the air leaving his lungs in a ragged rush. "It’s just… the details are completely skewed. It’s like we’re watching two different movies."

[story 2]

Sarah tapped her pen against the laminated table surface, a rapid, uneven rhythm that betrayed her mounting frustration. She picked up the napkin, folding it into a tight, aggressive square before shoving it into her pocket. "I don't get it," she muttered, her voice clipped and low. She looked across the table at Ben, whose expression was one of mild confusion. "I have a crystal-clear memory of the night we went to the beach. I was the one who accidentally knocked over the cooler, soaking everything. You were the one calmly helping me gather the shells." Ben shifted in his seat, rubbing the back of his neck. "But I remember feeling like I was the one who had to wrestle the rogue wave back in. I distinctly recall you being the one who was yelling instructions at me about which direction to run." Sarah pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal on tile sounding unnecessarily loud. She stood up abruptly, needing space. "This is exhausting. We can’t even agree on what happened."

[story 3]

Mark paced the length of his small apartment, his movements jerky and agitated. He stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights. He clenched his fists until his knuckles were white, the tension radiating up his arms. "This is absurd," he breathed out, the words tasting sour. He turned to Chloe, his eyes narrowed. "Look, I remember the argument perfectly. I was the one who slammed the door shut after you walked out. I remember you storming off, looking utterly defeated." Chloe crossed her arms over her chest, a faint smirk playing on her lips, though her eyes held a flicker of weariness. "That’s not what I experienced, Mark. I remember you trying to reason with me, desperately trying to de-escalate things. I distinctly recall *me* being the one who ended the conversation, feeling like I had to walk away first." Mark let out a short, sharp sound—a frustrated huff—and ran both hands through his hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically smooth out the confusion churning inside him. "Why is it so impossible to align our recollections?"
